target: data/wins.json WIN-010 — two targeted patches resolving ISS-1420 (internal contradiction between P1/P2 on whether dome has a magnetic-coupling equation) and ISS-1421 (P7 clean-external-test discriminator mis-identified as shadow coverage). Also tightens the finding field to concede the dome's scaling equation exists and the FSF station-to-station derivation is real, while keeping the critique on absolute-magnitude observation-anchoring.
patch_instructions: Three edits to the WIN with id='010' in data/wins.json. (1) Replace finding field (full-string swap, verified unique). (2) In detail_verdict_text, replace the unique P1 sentence 4 with the new-first-principles wording. (3) In detail_verdict_text, replace the unique P7 three-sentence block with the reframed discriminator block. All three anchors were verified to occur exactly once before writing this EXP. No other WIN-010 fields (claim, verdict, detail_claim, detail_evidence, detail_extra, tldr_evidence, tldr_verdict, code_analysis) are touched.

patches.before: Non-discriminating: Chapman 1933 + modern Sq models (Kim & Chang 2018) predict eclipse geomagnetic signals without dome geometry. Dome has no eclipse-magnetic coupling equation; its FSF formula uses the 2017 BOU observation as its own baseline — the absolute signal magnitude is observation-anchored, not dome-derived.
patches.after: Non-discriminating: Chapman 1933 + modern Sq models (Kim & Chang 2018) predict eclipse geomagnetic signals without dome geometry. The dome publishes a scaling equation (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) and derives the station-to-station FSF = H(r)/r ratio from its own geometry (Pearson r ≈ 0.9999); what it does not derive is the absolute magnitude at the Boulder anchor, which is imported from the 2017 observation and reused as the 2026 baseline — so the predicted signal is observation-anchored, not dome-derived.
patches.rationale: c6 Hole 5 recommendation + WIN-068 c9 Hole 1/2 parallel fix. Current finding says 'Dome has no eclipse-magnetic coupling equation' which (a) reads as the opposite of what detail_verdict_text P2 concedes (dome does publish delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) and (b) after the EXP-220 FSF-geometry concession, overclaims by implying no dome-derived equation exists at all. New finding concedes (i) the equation exists, (ii) the ratio derivation is real at Pearson r ≈ 0.9999, and (iii) keeps the critique on the absolute-magnitude import from 2017 as the non-discriminating piece. Mirrors the tightening WIN-068 c9 recommends for its own finding.

patches.before: The dome has no equation linking eclipses to magnetic perturbations; it simply observes that the signal exists and claims it.
patches.after: The dome has no first-principles equation that derives the eclipse magnitude from its own parameters; its published formula delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF takes the magnitude as an input from the 2017 observation it is trying to predict.
patches.rationale: c6 Hole 1 recommendation verbatim. Closes the internal P1/P2 contradiction a dome defender can quote (P1: dome has no equation; P2: here is the dome's equation). The intended point — no first-principles equation deriving magnitude from dome parameters, only a formula that imports magnitude from observation — is now on the page.

patches.before: Because FSF is now confirmed to track H(r)/r, the open question is whether 2026 observations track FSF more tightly than they track eclipse shadow coverage (Besselian-elements geometry, the standard-model prediction). Kim & Chang's ensemble shows the Y-component and X/Z/F responses track shadow geometry at magnetic latitudes 30°–50°, not disc-radius geometry. We will update this WIN when the 2026 INTERMAGNET data arrives; if delta_Z tracks shadow coverage more tightly than FSF, the dome's scaling is post-hoc; if FSF beats coverage on a clean out-of-sample test, something non-trivial will need explaining.
patches.after: Because FSF is now confirmed to track H(r)/r, the open question is whether 2026 residuals — (observed delta_Z) / (baseline × coverage), after factoring out the shadow-coverage term both models share — track the dome's FSF = H(r)/r profile (a function of geographic latitude through dome disc radius) more tightly than they track a magnetic-latitude scaling consistent with Kim & Chang's 30°–50° response zone. Both the dome formula delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF and a standard-physics prediction ≈ baseline × coverage × f(mag_lat) include coverage as a factor, so coverage is not the discriminator — the real fingerprint is geographic-latitude FSF versus magnetic-latitude f(mag_lat). We will update this WIN when the 2026 INTERMAGNET data arrives; if residuals track magnetic-latitude scaling more tightly than FSF, the dome's geographic-latitude scaling is effectively a proxy for the mag-lat Sq response; if FSF beats mag-lat scaling on a clean out-of-sample test, something non-trivial will need explaining.
patches.rationale: c6 Hole 2 recommendation expanded. The old P7 set up 'FSF vs shadow coverage' as the discriminator, but coverage is a shared factor in both the dome formula (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) and a standard-physics prediction (≈ baseline × coverage × f(mag_lat)) — so coverage is not the discriminator. New P7 frames the discriminator correctly as geographic-latitude FSF (H(r)/r, tied to dome disc radius) vs magnetic-latitude f(mag_lat) (Kim & Chang 30°–50° response zone), and defines the residual to divide out the shared coverage factor. Also updates the closing if/then so the pre-registered test is FSF vs mag-lat, not FSF vs coverage.

holes_addressed.hole: ISS-1420 / c6 Hole 1 (moderate) — Internal contradiction P1/P2
holes_addressed.description: P1 asserts 'dome has no equation linking eclipses to magnetic perturbations' while P2 concedes the dome publishes delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF and derives FSF = H(r)/r. A defender can grep P1 alone and claim the review is self-contradictory.
holes_addressed.fix: Rewrite P1 last sentence to 'no first-principles equation that derives the eclipse magnitude from its own parameters' — preserves the intended critique while owning the formula's existence. Finding field tightened in parallel.

holes_addressed.hole: ISS-1421 / c6 Hole 2 (moderate) — P7 discriminator mis-identified
holes_addressed.description: P7 stages the 2026-08-12 eclipse as a test of 'FSF vs shadow coverage.' But the dome formula already includes coverage; a standard-physics prediction would also scale by coverage. The real discriminator between dome and globe is not coverage — it is geographic-latitude FSF (dome disc radius) versus magnetic-latitude scaling (Kim & Chang 30°–50° response zone). A defender will correctly note we set up a straw discriminator.
holes_addressed.fix: Rewrite P7 to define the residual (observed delta_Z divided by baseline × coverage), state the real discriminator as FSF-vs-mag-lat scaling, and update the closing if/then accordingly. The pre-registered 2026 test now has real bite.

holes_addressed.hole: WIN-068 c9 Hole 1 (critical) — Finding-field retracted-framing parallel
holes_addressed.description: WIN-068 c9 flagged that WIN-010 finding carries the same factual error as WIN-068 (presenting a characterization we have retracted in detail_verdict_text). Same retraction needs propagating.
holes_addressed.fix: Finding now matches WIN-068 c9 recommended tightening: concede the equation exists and the ratio is derived; keep the critique on absolute-magnitude observation-anchoring.

new_evidence_added: Magnetic-latitude f(mag_lat) vs geographic-latitude FSF as the real discriminator (derived from Kim & Chang 2018 30°–50° response zone cited elsewhere in the WIN — no new citation needed).
Explicit formulation that both models share the coverage factor (derived from the dome formula in P2 and standard-physics structure in P1 — no new citation needed).

anticipated_objections.objection: The new finding is longer and more equivocal than the old one — doesn't that weaken the summary?
anticipated_objections.response: It is ~60 words longer but factually more accurate post-EXP-220. The old finding ran ahead of the detail text's concession, opening the review to a 'your own detail contradicts your own finding' attack. The new finding is the shortest form that concedes what we now concede while retaining the operative critique (absolute magnitude is observation-anchored). WIN-068 c9 asks for the parallel tightening on WIN-068's finding; keeping the two siblings aligned matters more than keeping either one terse.

anticipated_objections.objection: Rewriting P7 changes the claim the review makes about what the 2026-08-12 eclipse will test. Aren't we moving the goalposts?
anticipated_objections.response: The 2026-08-12 test has always been the real external check — what was wrong was the old P7 naming coverage as the opposing hypothesis. The test itself (2026 observed delta_Z at nine stations against dome's FSF table) is unchanged; what changes is the competing prediction we compare against (magnetic-latitude scaling per Kim & Chang, not bare shadow coverage). This is a sharpening, not a goalpost-move — coverage would have scaled both predictions and therefore would not have discriminated.

anticipated_objections.objection: The new P7 asks readers to compute a residual ((observed delta_Z) / (baseline × coverage)). Isn't that too technical for the reader-facing text?
anticipated_objections.response: The residual formulation is the only way to isolate the discriminating factor when both predictions share the coverage term. The alternative — leaving the old coverage-vs-FSF framing — reads as a category error to anyone who reads both models' formulas. The technical phrasing is one sentence; the operational conclusion (FSF-vs-mag-lat is what 2026 decides) is plain.

anticipated_objections.objection: Why not also take c6 Holes 3, 4, 5, 6 in the same patch?
anticipated_objections.response: Hole 3 (unsupported 'any smooth monotonic H(r)' claim) and Hole 4 (0.1%/1.8% precision slip) are separate logical units with their own anchors and would widen the anchor footprint, raising conflict risk. Hole 5 (Chapman 1933 overclaim in .finding) is partially absorbed by the new finding (which no longer relies on 'Chapman 1933 predicts exact magnitude'). Hole 6 (any-H(r) assertion refinement) and Hole 7 (OTS nuance in post-hoc paragraph) are all sentence-level tweaks that can be bundled into a follow-up EXP. Hole 8 (triple-count 69 vs current dome count) is a decider-integration concern, not an analyst prose patch.

kernel_of_truth: The c6 curmudgeon review is right that the old P1 and finding over-claimed in a way that reads as inconsistent with the (correct) P2 concession, and right that the old P7 set up a straw discriminator by pitting FSF against a factor that both models share. The kernel of truth in the dome defender position is that the dome DOES publish an equation and DOES derive FSF — we now concede both explicitly. What stays is that the equation imports its most important number (absolute magnitude at Boulder) from the very observation it is predicting, and that the discriminating fingerprint is geographic-latitude FSF versus magnetic-latitude scaling — a test 2026-08-12 will actually adjudicate.
cross_win_consistency_flag: WIN-068 finding still uses retracted 'empirically calibrated' language per WIN-068 c9 Hole 1. WIN-068 will need its own finding patch in parallel (not in scope for this EXP — WIN-068 c9 flagged it as critical and it should be queued as its own issue or EXP). Paragraph 7 of WIN-010 now introduces magnetic-latitude-vs-geographic-latitude framing that should be mirrored into WIN-068 detail_verdict_text paragraph 3 if/when WIN-068 gets its own rewrite; flagging for the decider.
out_of_scope_deliberately: c6 Hole 3 (moderate) — any-smooth-monotonic-H(r) claim lacks in-text demonstration. Should be its own follow-up patch with either a Gaussian/linear/power-law empirical footnote or a softening of the 'any H(r)' phrasing.

c6 Hole 4 (minor) — 0.1% at seven stations / 1.8% worst quantitative-precision slip against independent recompute (0.6% at seven stations / 2.78% worst). Should be its own micro-patch — two-word substitution in P2.
c6 Hole 5 (minor) — Chapman 1933 overclaim in .finding already mostly absorbed by the new finding, but specific phrasing tweak ('Chapman predicts exact magnitude') elsewhere if it recurs.
c6 Hole 6 (minor) — P3 sentence adding that dome H(r) was fitted to sun/moon altitude calibration targets (not eclipse data). Should be its own additive micro-patch.
c6 Hole 7 (minor) — OTS nuance in post-hoc paragraph. Should be a one-sentence follow-up patch that adds the explicit 'OTS anchors observation commits, not pre-data prediction commits' framing.
c6 Hole 8 (minor) — triple-count 69 number consistency across Parts 2b/3. Decider integration concern, not an analyst patch.
propagation_note: ISS-1398 flagged the EXP-220 retraction needs propagating across WIN-010/025/068 finding fields. WIN-068 finding propagation is blocked on its own parallel EXP. WIN-025 parallel catchup lives in the companion EXP-229 (this run) which rewrites WIN-025 detail_verdict_text to use Section 1.8 (Coordinate Collapse) vocabulary and concede FSF-as-geometric-derivation in the cluster-consistent way.
